content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED
blyl8702.1(a)C4?

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 19, 1992


Minna Williams
Williams and Williams
320 Fourth Street
Marysville, CA 95901

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-92-140

Dear Ms. Williams:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Mike Bluett concerning potential conflict-of-interest
situations which may arise under the Political Reform Act (the
"Act")¹ if Mr. Bluett is elected to the Yuba County Board of
Supervisors. Since your advice request does not refer to a
specific governmental decision, but instead seeks general
guidance, we are treating your request as one for informal
assistance.²

QUESTION

If Mr. Bluett is elected to the Yuba County Board of
Supervisors, what types of conflicts of interests will arise by
virtue of his spouse's position as elected Yuba County Assessor?

CONCLUSION

The position of the spouse of Mr. Bluett as Yuba County
Assessor will not result in conflicts of interest for Mr. Bluett
unless a decision will result in the hiring, firing, promotion,
demotion, or disciplining of his spouse, or the establishment of a

___________________________
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-140
Page 2


salary for his spouse which is different from salaries paid to
other employees of the county in the same job classification or
position.

DISCUSSION

Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he
has a financial interest. By the express terms of the
section, the prohibition is triggered by the potential effects of
specific decisions. Therefore, while Section 87100 might require
disqualification with respect to one or more decisions, it would
not preclude any person from holding any public office.

Section 87103 defines a financial interest as follows:

An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:

* * *

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(c) and (d).

You stated that Mr. Bluett's spouse is a salaried employee of
Yuba County. Section 82030 which defines "income" provides that
the income of an individual also includes any community property
interest in the income of a spouse. Thus, it could be argued that
Ms. Bluett's income with the county would result in the county,
and the county assessor's office being economic interests.

However, salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem
received from a state, local or federal government agency is
expressly exempted from the definition of "income" for purposes of
the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2); Fatland Advice Letter,

Our File No. I-92-140
Page 3


No. I-89-419.) In addition, a local government agency is not a
"business entity" as defined in the Act. A business entity is an
organization or enterprise operated for profit. (Section 82005.)
Thus, neither Mr. Bluett or his spouse would have an economic
interest in the county under the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act.

A conflict of interest may still exist, however, where a
governmental decision before Mr. Bluett will result in the
personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of Mr. Bluett or
his immediate family increasing or decreasing by $250. This is
the case if one of the source of the increase of accruals.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).) Thus, this provision contemplates
even increases and decreases to income received from governmental
agencies as being within the parameters of the regulation.
(Torrance Advice Letter, No. I-89-142.)

However, subdivision (c) of Regulation 18702.1 provides an
exception to this rule. Despite financial effects caused by a
decision on the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities
of Mr. Bluett or his immediate family of $250, Mr. Bluett will not
have to disqualify himself if the decision will only affect the
salary, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses Mr. Bluett or his
spouse receives from a state or local government agency. Thus, if
the decisions of the board of supervisors will affect the salary
of Mr. Bluett's spouse, Mr. Bluett will not be precluded from
participating in the budget decision. However, the exception in
Regulation 18702.1(c) does not apply to decisions to hire, fire,
promote, demote, or discipline an official's spouse, or to set a
salary for an official's spouse which is different from salaries
paid to other employees of the spouse's agency in the same job
classification or position.

I trust these general guidelines have addressed your
concerns. If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.3

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
    Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

_________________________
3 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
